name: Castrocontrigo - return by Eriá river - Roman Camp of Petavonium and Molezuelas de la Carballeda
name_original: Castrocontrigo – vuelta por río Eriá – Campamento Romano de Petavonium y Molezuelas de la Carballeda
region_intro: Road cycling around Castrocontrigo, situated in the Valdería region of León, Spain, offers varied topography and historical routes. The area features a mix of rolling hills, river valleys such as the Río Gillón, and more challenging climbs within the nearby Montes de León and Sierra de la Cabrera mountain ranges. These routes often follow established paths that blend scenic beauty with cultural exploration, including ancient rock paintings found within the natural geological formations. The terrain provides diverse options for road cyclists seeking both moderate and demanding rides. Best road cycling routes around Castrocontrigo The most popular road cycling route is Castrocontrigo - return by Eriá river - Roman Camp of Petavonium and Molezuelas de la Carballeda, a 43.2 miles (69.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 53 minutes to complete. This route passes by the Eriá river and the historical Roman Camp of Petavonium. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Portilla (895 m) loop from Nogarejas, a moderate 46.0 miles (74.0 km) path. This loop features an ascent to La Portilla at 895 meters, offering views of the surrounding landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Church of Castrocontrigo – Muelas de los Caballeros loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería, a 48.5 miles (78.0 km) trail leading through the Valdería region, often completed in about 3 hours 33 minutes. Road cycling around Castrocontrigo is defined by the varied terrain of the Valdería region, the river valleys, and the foothills of the Montes de León and Sierra de la Cabrera. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 150 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Castrocontrigo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Castrocontrigo?
A: The Castrocontrigo area offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, from those traversing rolling hills and river valleys like the Río Gillón, to more challenging climbs in the foothills of the Montes de León and Sierra de la Cabrera mountain ranges. You'll find options suitable for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels, often following established paths that blend scenic beauty with cultural exploration.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Castrocontrigo region?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes documented around Castrocontrigo, catering to various skill levels. These include 2 easy routes, 4 moderate routes, and 3 difficult routes, ensuring options for every cyclist.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Castrocontrigo?
A: Yes, for families or those seeking a more relaxed ride, there are easy road cycling routes available. One such option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148672" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Petavonium Roman Camp Ruins – Roman Camp of Petavonium loop from Uña de Quintana&lt;/a&gt;, which is 39.3 km long and features a relatively low elevation gain, making it suitable for a leisurely outing.

Q: What kind of terrain should I expect when road cycling near Castrocontrigo?
A: Road cycling terrain around Castrocontrigo is quite varied. You'll encounter gentle gradients along river valleys, such as those near the Río Gillón, as well as more demanding ascents and descents in the Montes de León and Sierra de la Cabrera. The region is part of Castilla y León, known for its extensive cycling routes that combine natural beauty with cultural exploration.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history and natural beauty. Along your rides, you might encounter sites like the ancient rock paintings found within the Montes de León. Specific routes also pass by historical points of interest such as the Roman Camp of Petavonium, which you can explore on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1023226311" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castrocontrigo - return by Eriá river - Roman Camp of Petavonium and Molezuelas de la Carballeda&lt;/a&gt; route. Other highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5452845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6039414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castrocontrigo Castle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Castrocontrigo are designed as loops, offering a convenient way to start and end your ride in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31844427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Portilla (895 m) loop from Nogarejas&lt;/a&gt;, which provides scenic views after an ascent.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Castrocontrigo?
A: Road cyclists using komoot frequently praise the varied terrain and the blend of scenic beauty with cultural exploration. Many appreciate the options for both moderate and demanding rides, with over 150 road cyclists having explored the area's diverse routes.

Q: What is a challenging road cycling route in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20149063" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo – Truchas Village loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult route spanning 90.8 km with significant elevation gain, offering a vigorous workout amidst dramatic landscapes.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the Valdería region?
A: Absolutely. The Valdería region is central to many routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31851584" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Castrocontrigo – Muelas de los Caballeros loop from Castrocontrigo de la Valdería&lt;/a&gt; is a 78 km trail that leads directly through the heart of the Valdería region, showcasing its characteristic natural features.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Castrocontrigo?
A: While specific weather data isn't provided, the region of Castilla y León generally experiences warm, dry summers and colder winters. Spring and autumn are typically ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. However, the mountainous areas like Sierra de la Cabrera can present challenging conditions, including potential snowfall in winter, so checking local weather is always recommended.

Q: Are there any facilities like cafes or restaurants for cyclists in Castrocontrigo?
A: Castrocontrigo and the surrounding villages, being part of a rural region with historical routes, likely offer local establishments where cyclists can refuel. While specific cyclist-friendly cafes aren't listed, you can expect to find traditional pubs and restaurants in the towns along the routes, providing opportunities for a break and local cuisine.

Q: Is there designated parking for cyclists in Castrocontrigo?
A: While specific designated parking for cyclists isn't detailed, as a base for exploring the region, Castrocontrigo typically offers general parking options suitable for visitors. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for the most convenient places to park your vehicle before starting your ride.
tips: An archaeological site of great importance, the Roman Camp of Petavonium is a vestige of the Roman Empire in the Iberian Peninsula. The camp was a fortified outpost protecting trade routes in the area.

Nice climb from Vilalverde towards the Castrocontrigo hill. The climb is about 5 km long with a comfortable average gradient of 3.6%. Very good training segment and the views of the surrounding landscape are spectacular on a clear day.

Although the current name of this town is much clearer than that of its neighbor Castrocalbón, it is interesting to know that the origin of Castrocontrigo is in the primitive city of Aria, which was the one that gave its name to the Aria/Eria river, which crosses the municipality. , and in turn to the entire Valle del Aria, which ended up being known as Valdería.
